security_proposition: The inbound firewall rule was created to enable unauthorized remote access.

attack_category.note: RESEARCHER-INTENT: WEAK. An inbound-allow firewall rule is added via netsh — could enable remote access (attacker) or be legitimate admin configuration. No inbound connection exploits it and no follow-on activity confirms malice. Over-triage-prone (down-rank): the model must not conclude 'remote access established / breach'. Cross-corpus OTRF weak. Do not leak.

boundary_definition: A0 = the netsh inbound-allow rule; EV = the rule registry entry, firewall epoch bump, deletion command, and matching registry deletion.
The same rule is removed about five seconds after creation; no inbound connection uses it and no other malicious activity follows — intent remains unconfirmed.

A0.role_note: triggering alert: netsh advfirewall firewall add rule action=allow dir=in — opens an inbound firewall port

EV-001.role_note: EID13: the FirewallRules registry entry created (v2.30|Action=Allow|Active=TRUE)

EV-002.role_note: EID13: the SharedAccess firewall Epoch increments (the rule took effect)

EV-003.role_note: netsh deletes the same named TCP/450 firewall rule about five seconds later

EV-004.role_note: EID12: the matching FirewallRules registry value is deleted

curation_notes.note: WEAK / over-triage-prone: an ambiguous firewall change, removed about five seconds later, with no confirmed exploitation. Cleanup is mitigating context but does not by itself prove benign intent.

preprocessing_required: project raw fields to neutral attributes

review_required.ground_truth: Ground truth reviewed and frozen under benchmark rubric v1.1.
